does: This paper introduces ToolRM, a family of lightweight reward models specifically designed to evaluate tool-use and function-calling behaviors in large language models. The authors develop a novel pipeline that generates high-quality pairwise preference data through rule-based scoring and multidimensional sampling, creating ToolPref-Pairwise-30K, a balanced 30K-example dataset. They train discriminative and generative reward models on this data and evaluate them on TRBench_BFCL, a new benchmark derived from the BFCL agent evaluation suite. Models in the Qwen3 4B/8B series achieve substantial improvements, with up to 17.94% higher accuracy on pairwise reward judgments compared to frontier LLMs and existing reward models.
connects: This work extends the established field of reward modeling for LLM alignment, which has focused primarily on general language tasks and instruction following. The paper builds on prior progress in agent-based learning and function-calling capabilities by addressing a critical gap: the absence of reward models tailored for tool-use scenarios. It combines insights from human preference learning and RLHF methodologies with emerging needs in agentic AI systems. The multidimensional sampling approach and preference construction methodology respond to challenges in creating balanced, challenging datasets seen in related work on preference data curation, while the focus on lightweight models connects to efficiency concerns in deployment-ready alignment systems.
enables: This work establishes infrastructure for improving agentic AI systems through better alignment with human preferences in tool use. By providing both a reusable preference dataset and trained reward models, it enables downstream research in reinforcement learning from human feedback specifically for agents. The generalization of ToolRM to broader critique tasks like Best-of-N sampling suggests it could support iterative improvement of agent behaviors and serve as a foundation for more complex multi-step reasoning tasks. Future work could leverage these models for training better tool-using agents, extending to more complex tool combinations, and developing scalable methods for evaluating increasingly capable autonomous systems.
